title: Evictions and Premises Clearance – Court Bailiff Proceedings in Poland
description: Learn how eviction proceedings are carried out by a court bailiff in Poland. Legal basis, procedure, tenant protections, and social housing rights.
content:
What Is a Bailiff Eviction?

A bailiff eviction is the compulsory clearance of a dwelling or premises carried out by a court bailiff (komornik sądowy) on the basis of a final court judgment bearing an enforcement clause. The bailiff acts strictly within the framework of applicable law, ensuring the dignity of evicted persons is respected at all times.

Legal Basis

Eviction proceedings in Poland are governed primarily by:

  • The Code of Civil Procedure (Art. 1046 CCP) – concerning enforcement of the obligation to vacate premises,
  • The Act on the Protection of Tenants' Rights of 21 June 2001 – special protections for tenants and entitlement to social housing,
  • The Act on Court Bailiffs of 22 March 2018.

When Can Eviction Take Place?

Eviction is only permissible on the basis of an enforcement title, most commonly:

  • a court judgment ordering the occupant to vacate and surrender the premises,
  • a notarial deed with voluntary submission to enforcement,
  • a court order or settlement agreement containing an obligation to vacate.

The Eviction Procedure

1. Filing the enforcement application – the creditor (e.g. the landlord or property owner) submits an application to the bailiff's office together with the enforcement title.

2. Notice to vacate voluntarily – the bailiff calls on the debtor to voluntarily vacate the premises within a specified period.

3. Verification of social housing entitlement – before proceeding, the bailiff checks whether the court has stayed execution pending the municipality's provision of social housing.

4. Forced clearance – if the debtor does not vacate voluntarily, the bailiff carries out the forced removal of persons and belongings from the premises.

5. Handover to the creditor – following clearance, the bailiff draws up a record and formally hands over the premises to the creditor.

Tenant Protections

Polish law provides special protection against eviction from 1 November to 31 March each year (the protection period). During this time, the bailiff may not carry out an eviction to temporary accommodation or a homeless shelter. This protection does not apply in cases of domestic violence.

Social Housing

If the court's eviction judgment grants the debtor a right to social housing, the eviction is stayed until the municipality provides such housing. The bailiff suspends proceedings until this obligation is fulfilled.

Costs

The costs of eviction proceedings are generally borne by the debtor and include the bailiff's fee, transport and storage of belongings, and other related expenses.

metaDescription: How does a court bailiff carry out an eviction in Poland? Procedure, tenant rights, social housing and costs. Bailiff's Office No. IX, Harvey Specter, Otwock.
